recommended_focus.name: Flask Route Smoke Test Suite
recommended_focus.why: Protects KDT OS from breaking as it grows by proving important routes still load.
recommended_focus.first_step: Create tests/test_routes.py and add one test that checks the home route returns 200.
recommended_focus.proof: Upload test_routes.py and a screenshot or copied output from pytest.

steps.title: Create test folder
steps.instructions: Create a tests folder and a test_routes.py file.

steps.title: Identify routes
steps.instructions: Write down 3 important routes that should return successfully.
steps.expected_output: README or test file lists the routes.

steps.title: Create first smoke test
steps.instructions: Write one test that checks the home page returns status code 200.

steps.title: Add second route test
steps.instructions: Add a second route test for a key page like /command_center or /project_coach.

steps.title: Run pytest
steps.instructions: Run python -m pytest or document the exact command you would use.
steps.expected_output: Terminal shows test result or README explains blocker.

steps.title: Record failures
steps.instructions: If any route fails, write the route and error in README.md.
steps.expected_output: Failures are documented or marked none.

steps.title: Add proof summary
steps.instructions: Write what smoke tests protect against.

steps.title: Submit final proof
steps.instructions: Zip tests and README.

next_step.title: Create test folder
next_step.instructions: Create a tests folder and a test_routes.py file.

plans.name: System Health Dashboard
plans.why: Turns your server troubleshooting experience into a reusable tool for CPU, RAM, disk, and service checks.
plans.first_step: Create a Python script that outputs fake or real CPU, memory, disk, and service status as OK/WARNING.
plans.proof: Upload code and a screenshot of the dashboard or terminal health report.

steps.title: Create project folder
steps.instructions: Create a folder named system_health_dashboard with README.md and starter files.

steps.title: Write project purpose
steps.instructions: In README.md, explain what System Health Dashboard does and which skills it trains: Health Monitoring, Python, Dashboard/UI, File Handling.

steps.title: Build the smallest working version
steps.instructions: Create a Python script that outputs fake or real CPU, memory, disk, and service status as OK/WARNING.

steps.title: Add one real input
steps.instructions: Let the user enter or change at least one piece of data.
steps.expected_output: Input changes the output or saved data.

steps.title: Save or display result
steps.instructions: Show the result clearly or save it to a file/database/localStorage.

steps.title: Clean the UI/output
steps.instructions: Make the output readable and label the important values.

steps.title: Write proof summary
steps.instructions: Write 3-5 sentences explaining what worked and what skill improved.

steps.title: Submit final proof
steps.instructions: Zip the project and include proof artifacts.

next_step.title: Create project folder
next_step.instructions: Create a folder named system_health_dashboard with README.md and starter files.

plans.name: Workout Database App
plans.why: Builds the exact SQLite CRUD skills KDT OS keeps detecting as useful while staying small and personal.
plans.first_step: Create a folder with app.py and workouts.json or workouts.db, then add one workout record with exercise, reps, sets, and date.
plans.proof: Upload a ZIP showing create, list, update, and delete working on workout records.

steps.title: Create project folder
steps.instructions: Create a folder named workout_database_app with README.md and app.py.
steps.expected_output: Folder exists with README.md and app.py.
steps.proof_required: Screenshot or ZIP showing the folder.

steps.title: Design the workout record
steps.instructions: Write the fields you will store: exercise, sets, reps, date, and notes.
steps.expected_output: README.md lists the fields and explains each one.

steps.title: Create the database/table
steps.instructions: Use SQLite or a JSON starter file to create storage for workout records.
steps.expected_output: Storage file exists and has a workouts structure/table.
steps.proof_required: Screenshot or code showing the table/structure.

steps.title: Add create behavior
steps.instructions: Add one function or form action that saves a workout record.
steps.expected_output: At least one workout can be added.
steps.proof_required: Code plus screenshot/output showing the added workout.

steps.title: Add list behavior
steps.instructions: Display all saved workout records.

steps.title: Add update behavior
steps.instructions: Change one saved workout record.
steps.expected_output: An existing workout can be edited.

steps.title: Add delete behavior
steps.instructions: Remove one workout record.
steps.expected_output: A workout can be deleted and no longer appears.

steps.title: Submit final proof
steps.instructions: Zip the folder and write what this project proved about SQLite/CRUD.

next_step.title: Create project folder
next_step.instructions: Create a folder named workout_database_app with README.md and app.py.
next_step.expected_output: Folder exists with README.md and app.py.
next_step.proof_required: Screenshot or ZIP showing the folder.

plans.name: JavaScript Form Saver
plans.why: Builds browser app confidence by saving form input without needing a backend yet.
plans.first_step: Create a form with name, note, and category fields, then save one entry to localStorage.
plans.proof: Upload ZIP and screenshot showing saved data still appears after refresh.

plans.name: API Weather Lookup App
plans.why: Turns API and JavaScript gaps into a visible browser app with an easy proof target.
plans.first_step: Create index.html, style.css, and script.js with a button that displays sample weather JSON.
plans.proof: Upload a ZIP and screenshot showing city, temperature, and condition displayed after button click.

steps.title: Create browser files
steps.instructions: Create index.html, style.css, and script.js in one project folder.
steps.proof_required: Screenshot or ZIP showing the files.

steps.title: Build the page structure
steps.instructions: Add a title, button, and result box to index.html.
steps.expected_output: Button and result box are visible.

steps.title: Connect JavaScript
steps.instructions: Link script.js and select the button/result elements.

steps.title: Add click event
steps.instructions: Use addEventListener so the button changes the result text.

steps.title: Load sample weather data
steps.instructions: Create a sample weather object or fetch sample JSON and display city, temperature, and condition.
steps.expected_output: The three weather fields display on the page.

steps.title: Style the card
steps.instructions: Make the result box readable and clean on desktop and narrow width.
steps.expected_output: Page is readable at normal and narrow sizes.

steps.title: Write explanation
steps.instructions: In README.md, explain what fetch/API-style data means and what your button does.

steps.title: Submit final proof
steps.instructions: Zip the project and include screenshots.
steps.expected_output: ZIP is ready with files and proof.

next_step.title: Create browser files
next_step.instructions: Create index.html, style.css, and script.js in one project folder.
next_step.proof_required: Screenshot or ZIP showing the files.
